Orion.description: The Hunter, one of the most recognizable constellations. Anchored by the bright shoulders Betelgeuse and Bellatrix, the feet Rigel and Saiph, and the three-star Belt Mintaka–Alnilam–Alnitak.

Big Dipper.description: The seven-star asterism in Ursa Major (Great Bear), one of the most familiar shapes in the northern sky. Dubhe and Merak are the 'pointers' that aim toward Polaris.

Cassiopeia.description: The Queen, a distinctive 'W' (or 'M' depending on orientation) of five bright stars in the northern circumpolar sky.

Crux.description: The Southern Cross, the smallest constellation but one of the most recognizable, depicted on several southern-hemisphere flags. Acrux and Gacrux form the long axis; Mimosa and Imai form the cross-bar.

Northern Cross.description: The cruciform asterism of Cygnus the Swan, with Deneb at the tail and Albireo at the beak. One leg of the Summer Triangle along with Vega and Altair.

Lyra.description: The Lyre, a small but bright constellation dominated by Vega — the brightest star in the northern celestial hemisphere and one corner of the Summer Triangle.

Leo.description: The Lion, with the curved 'Sickle' asterism marking the lion's mane and head, and a triangle of stars (Denebola, Zosma, Chertan) marking the hindquarters and tail.

Scorpius.description: The Scorpion, with red supergiant Antares at its heart and a long curving tail ending in the stinger stars Shaula and Lesath. One of the few constellations that genuinely resembles its namesake.

Canis Major.description: The Greater Dog, hosting Sirius, the brightest star in the night sky. The body is outlined by Mirzam, Adhara, Wezen, and Aludra.

Canis Minor.description: The Lesser Dog, a tiny constellation whose only bright stars are Procyon (eighth-brightest in the sky) and Gomeisa.

Gemini.description: The Twins, with the heads of Castor and Pollux side by side and two parallel lines of stars trailing off representing their bodies.

Taurus.description: The Bull, with the V-shaped Hyades cluster forming the face, red giant Aldebaran as the glaring eye, and two long horns ending at Elnath and Tianguan.

Auriga.description: The Charioteer, a large pentagon of bright stars sharing Elnath with Taurus. Capella, the sixth-brightest star in the sky, dominates.

Perseus.description: The Hero, holding the severed head of Medusa (Algol, the 'Demon Star', a famous eclipsing binary). Mirfak anchors the central curve.

Andromeda.description: The Chained Princess. A chain of three bright stars — Alpheratz, Mirach, Almach — runs away from the Great Square of Pegasus.

Pegasus.description: The Winged Horse, marked by the Great Square (Markab, Scheat, Alpheratz, Algenib) forming the body. Enif marks the horse's nose.

Aries.description: The Ram. A small zodiacal constellation marked by a simple arc of three stars: Hamal, Sheratan, and Mesarthim.

Virgo.description: The Maiden, the second-largest constellation in the sky. Anchored by the blue supergiant Spica, with Porrima and Vindemiatrix extending the wheat-sheaf the figure carries.

Libra.description: The Scales, the only zodiac constellation named for an object rather than a creature. Zubenelgenubi ('southern claw') and Zubeneschamali ('northern claw') recall its former role as part of Scorpius.

Sagittarius.description: The Archer, whose brightest stars form the famous 'Teapot' asterism pointed toward the center of the Milky Way.

Capricornus.description: The Sea-Goat, a faint but ancient zodiacal figure forming a rough triangle in the southern sky.

Aquarius.description: The Water-Bearer, one of the oldest recognized constellations. Sadalmelik and Sadalsuud are the two luckiest stars in the sky by traditional Arabic names.

Aquila.description: The Eagle, with Altair at the head flanked by Tarazed and Alshain. Altair is the twelfth-brightest star in the sky and one corner of the Summer Triangle.

Delphinus.description: The Dolphin, a small diamond-shaped constellation. Sualocin and Rotanev encode Nicolaus Venator (Niccolò Cacciatore) when read backwards.

Boötes.description: The Herdsman, a kite-shaped constellation anchored by brilliant orange Arcturus, the fourth-brightest star in the sky.

Hercules.description: The Strong Man, a large but relatively faint constellation named for the mythological hero.

Ophiuchus.description: The Serpent-Bearer, a large constellation occasionally called the 13th zodiac sign since the ecliptic passes through it.

Draco.description: The Dragon, a long winding circumpolar constellation whose tail once contained the pole star Thuban ~4,800 years ago.

Cepheus.description: The King, a house-shaped constellation near the north celestial pole.

Cetus.description: The Sea Monster (or Whale), a large sprawling southern constellation from the Perseus myth.

Eridanus.description: The River, an enormous winding constellation beginning near Rigel (in Orion) and flowing down to brilliant Achernar in the far south.

Lepus.description: The Hare, crouching at the feet of Orion.

Hydra.description: The Water Snake, the largest of the 88 modern constellations by area. Alphard ('the solitary one') is its only bright star.

Centaurus.description: The Centaur, home to the closest stellar system to Sol (Alpha Centauri) and the second-brightest star in the constellation, Hadar.

Carina.description: The Keel of the ship Argo. Canopus is the second-brightest star in the night sky.

Vela.description: The Sails of the ship Argo, forming part of the 'False Cross' asterism often mistaken for the Southern Cross.

Grus.description: The Crane, a southern constellation introduced by Dutch navigators in the late 16th century.
